channel.title: maGaming RSS Feed - www.gamedeveloper.com
channel.description: A cleaned-up version of the original gaming feed for www.gamedeveloper.com

item.title: Hasbro cancels Dungeons & Dragons game led by Respawn veterans
item.content:encoded:
Image via Dungeons & Dragons and Giant Skull

The collaboration between Wizards of the Coast (WOTC), a Hasbro subsidiary, and developer Giant Skull, a studio founded by Respawn veteran Stig Asmussen, has come to a close less than a year after the announcement.

This has been confirmed by WOTC itself, which shared the news in a statement to Bloomberg. The companies were working on an action-adventure game set in the Dungeons & Dragons universe, which, at the time of its reveal, was deemed a "definitive moment in both companies' gaming ambitions."

As it turns out, WOTC pulled out of the deal earlier this year. "We assess concepts at every stage of development," a spokesperson told Bloomberg.

Moreover, the company added that, while it decided not to pursue an early concept from Giant Skull, it retains "great respect for Stig Asmussen and his team and value our ongoing relationship."

As for Asmussen himself, he told Bloomberg that he had enjoyed working with WOTC, and that he was talking to people there and at other companies about potential new publishing deals.

Related:Embracer chairman says cost control 'does not get enough respect' after presiding over years of layoffs

"Things are good at Giant Skull," he told the publication.

"Stig and the team at Giant Skull are exactly the type of exceptionally talented creators we want to work with, and I'm so happy to be reuniting with him on this new project," president of WOTC and digital gaming at Hasbro John Hight said back in June of last year.

Asmussen's team was established in 2024, formed by several Respawn Entertainment alumni and a number of former Fortnite developers.

When asked about his decision to leave a relatively stable operation at Respawn to pursue a new studio venture, Asmussen said the following: "One of my main mottos is 'no regrets. I didn't want to be in a situation where I decided to stay put and a year or two years from now, I'd be looking over my shoulder and be like 'what about that other thing I could have done?'"

Giant Skull's game isn't the only project that Hasbro has axed this year. Back in February, a company spokesperson confirmed the closure of Atomic Arcade, the studio established by WOTC and Hasbro. Yet, it also said the studio's unnamed Snake Eyes project has not been canceled.

About the Author

Contributing Editor, News, GameDeveloper.com

Diego Nicolás Argüello is a freelance journalist and critic from Argentina. Video games helped him to learn English, so now he covers them for places like The New York Times, NPR, Rolling Stone, and more. He also runs Into the Spine, a site dedicated to fostering and supporting new writers, and co-hosted Turnabout Breakdown, a podcast about the Ace Attorney series. He’s most likely playing a rhythm game as you read this.



https://www.gamedeveloper.com/business/hasbro-cancels-dungeons-dragons-game-led-by-respawn-veteran
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Embracer chairman says cost control 'does not get enough respect' after presiding over years of layoffs
item.content:encoded:
Embracer Group founder, chairman, and former CEO Lars Wingefors is mounting an eyebrow-raising defense of the embattled video game conglomerate as it prepares to spin off another round of studios. In an open letter to investors discussing the news, Wingefors commented on the tidal wave of layoffs and studio closures at the company that followed the collapse of a $2 billion investment (allegedly by Saudi Arabia-owned Savvy Games Group) back in 2023.

In the letter, he offered a mea culpa to investors disappointed by what he described as "negative value creation" that has taken place since 2020, expressing confidence that the new spinoff would prepare the company for financial success. Then he shared his view of Embracer's layoffs.

"Even if, for some, we have become closely associated with industry layoffs, the reality is that we have worked hard to retain as many people as possible through a very difficult period, while balancing the needs to drive a profitable business operation," he writes. "That reflects our commitment to our people, our portfolio of beloved IPs, and the promises we have made to entrepreneurs."

Related:'We've been a little bit too romantic:' Ustwo CEO says lowering development costs is now paramount

Those and other comments shed some light on how leadership at Embracer views the effectiveness of its (still-ongoing) rounds of cuts. Wingefors characterizes the cuts as being different from a "hard 'US Corporate style' headcount reduction," and that Embracer gave its subsidiary studios "a chance to prove themselves."

Further down the letter he appears to cast doubt on those critical of this path. "Cost control does not get enough respect, but over time it makes a bigger difference than many people realize," he adds. That statement was included alongside a list of "insights" detailing what the company has learned following years of divestments and layoffs.

These insights also include statements like: "Trust matters more than anything. Keep your word, treat people well, and never forget to be human," as well as "the businesses that have done best are often the ones that kept focusing on what already worked, without letting costs run away."

Wingefors has certainly faced his fair share of criticism for overseeing the mass layoffs and closure over the last few years—so much criticism in fact, that former fellow Embracer board member Matt Karch made an effort to stridently defend him in posts on LinkedIn and in an interview with Game Developer (Karch is now CEO of Saber Interactive, which spun off from Embracer in 2024). 

"I think you can blame Lars for maybe being a little bit naive that this gravy train would just continue," he said in another interview with IGN, alluding to the huge flow of player spending and external investment in the video game industry that followed the height of the COVID-19 pandemic. "But I think now you could admire them for making tough decisions and doing whatever they can to preserve as much of what they've built as they can in a fair and equitable way."

About the Author

Senior Editor, GameDeveloper.com

Bryant Francis is a writer, journalist, and narrative designer based in Boston, MA. He currently writes for Game Developer, a leading B2B publication for the video game industry. His credits include Proxy Studios' 4X strategy game Zephon, Iron Anchor Studios' Down With The Ship, and Amplitude Studio's 2017 game Endless Space 2.



https://www.gamedeveloper.com/business/embracer-ceo-says-cost-control-does-not-get-enough-respect-
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: UK publisher EdCorp breaks cover after signing Garbage Country
item.content:encoded:
Chris Kerr,

Senior Editor, News,GameDeveloper.com

May 20, 2026

1 Min Read

Former Coatsink founders and shareholders Eddie Beardsmore, Tom Beardsmore, Paul Crabb and Simon Launder have formed EdCorp, a new UK developer and indie publisher that will focus on bringing original and licensed titles to market. 

Notably, EdCorp intends to finance projects be offering what it described as "fair, dev-focused terms" to indies around the globe. 

"EdCorp will fund, market, and develop original and licensed IP/co-dev projects across PC and console," reads a press release. "The team is dedicated to supporting small, creatively driven teams that can bring new experiences to players worldwide and will do so with a variety of funding options, including up to 75 percent revenue share for developers after costs."

The Newcastle-based company is currently soliciting pitches and has already agreed to publish three projects, the first of which is upcoming post-apocalyptic exploration sim Garbage Country. As noted on the EdCorp website, All partners will retain control of their IP.

Related:Embracer chairman says cost control 'does not get enough respect' after presiding over years of layoffs

EdCorp CEO and co-founder Tom Beardsmore said the leadership team want to build a company that feels "genuine and fun." 

"We believe the downturn in the industry in recent years has cast a large shadow over independent developers' and their creative ambitions. It is our job to help rekindle that ambition and bring creative, inspiring projects to players," he added. 

During their time at Coatsink, the EdCorp team helped grow the company into one of the North East's leading game developers and publishers by working on notable titles such as Jurassic World Aftermath, Batman: Arkham Shadow (co-dev), Gang Beasts (co-dev), and Kingdom Two Crowns (co-dev).

Coatsink was acquired by Thunderful Group for almost $30 million in 2020. Last year, GamesIndustry.biz noted the publisher made significant cuts to survive in a "challenging co-development market.' 

About the Author

Senior Editor, News, GameDeveloper.com

Game Developer news editor Chris Kerr is an award-winning reporter with over a decade of experience in the game industry. His byline has appeared in notable print and digital publications including Edge, Stuff, Wireframe, International Business Times, and PocketGamer.biz. Throughout his career, Chris has covered major industry events including GDC, PAX Australia, Gamescom, Paris Games Week, and Develop Brighton. 



https://www.gamedeveloper.com/business/uk-publisher-edcorp-breaks-cover-after-signing-garbage-country
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot
